theme: The Carpenter Who Rebuilt the Harbor Town
text:
In July, a gorgeous southern light fell over the seashore town, and a lad named Tomas stood in the yard behind his cellar workshop. He was a carpenter by trade, habitual in his work, and the message that reached him that morning would change everything. The local economy had suffered a great loss; the old superstructure of the wharf had begun to creep and crumble, and the bottom-line of the council's budget looked bleak. The success of any repair was uncertain, but Tomas was not indifferent.

He loaded a barge with timber, a carton of nails, and a soda for the road. A migrant crew, lawful and hardworking, helped him stow the cargo. The shipping company had sent finished beams and planks fresh from a processing yard; the manufacturer's catalog, quoted at a cif price, promised they would not flatten or warp. Tomas would measure each one, for accurate measurement was, in his view, a requisite of any honest craft. He refused to portray cheap work as good; he had nothing but contempt for advertising that hid the exact truth, and he would enumerate every fault aloud rather than stay silent. A former apprentice, now a meat-shop owner, lent him an old mincer to grind paint pigment and a sack of crushed ore for ballast.

As they worked, a puppy crept near, and an old hostess from the inn brought clothes and a pan of food. The men did not grumble; even when a sudden clash of opinion threatened to creep into a quarrel, prudence and the foreman's authentic kindness restrained it. One worker tried to sneer at the slow pace, but Tomas would not let impatience curb the perfection he sought. "We must not impair the frame," he said, "nor inflict a hidden flaw that doom will later expose."

The scene was picturesque, almost like a mirage: vines on the southern wall, a robot crane lent by a modern firm, a wedge of light over the water. Tomas would wrap a splint of oak around a cracked post, compress the joint, and nail a carton-thin board to hide the seam. He would eliminate every weak point, encircle each pillar with iron, and reduce the risk of collapse. He would not be the cause of the harbor's downfall.

By nine that evening the men were awake but weary. A surgeon from the next avenue, summoned for a minor cut, praised their measurement and their nerve. The poison of doubt had no hold here. Tomas thought of the mercantile glory the town once knew, of the gaudy festivals, the wedding processions down the main avenue, the trail of lanterns along the pavement. He would liberate the town from decay. He picked up his nozzle and hose to clear the drain, flicked a flap of canvas over the fresh wood, and felt, despite the shameful neglect of years past, a clear and gorgeous hope. The language of good work, he knew, needs no translation; it speaks in every yard of solid timber. The robot crane hummed; the lad smiled; the harbor would live.

Later, in the town stadium, the mayor gave a ponderous monologue that some called epoch-making, though a blunt fisherman muttered, "Oh, fuck the speeches." Tomas, a former apprentice now a master, was decidedly calm. He would not be the judge of politics. He let his plans materialize on paper, enumerate each cost on a line marked cif for the imported steel and iron ore, and restrain his impatient hopes. The automatic saw whirred near a vine-covered fence. A neighbor used a mincer to prepare stuffing for a roast, and Tomas would cook a simple supper at home. He clutched a worn garment to his bosom against the night chill. This was no fiction, no idle processing of dreams; it was real work, and it was good.
translation:
七月，一片绚丽的南方光线洒在这座海滨小镇上，一个名叫托马斯的小伙子站在他地窖作坊后面的院子里。他以木匠为业，做事一向有条不紊，而那天早上传到他这里的消息将改变一切。当地经济遭受了巨大的损失；码头那座古老的上层建筑开始一点点下陷崩塌，议会预算的结余看上去一片黯淡。任何修缮能否成功都没有把握，但托马斯并不漠然。

他往一艘驳船上装了木料、一纸箱钉子，还有路上喝的一瓶汽水。一支合法又勤劳的外来工队帮他把货物装好。航运公司送来了成品横梁和加工好的木板；制造商的目录承诺它们不会被压平或翘曲。托马斯会一一测量，因为在他看来，精确的测量是任何诚实手艺的必备条件。他拒绝把劣质活儿粉饰成好活儿；对于那种隐瞒确切真相的广告，他只有轻蔑。

他们干活时，一只小狗悄悄凑近，客栈里一位上了年纪的女主人送来衣服和一锅食物。工人们没有抱怨；即便突然爆发的意见冲突险些演变成争吵，谨慎和工头那真诚的善意也将它克制住了。一个工人想要嘲笑这缓慢的进度，但托马斯不让急躁束缚他所追求的完美。"我们绝不能损害骨架，"他说，"也不能埋下某个迟早会被厄运揭露的隐患。"

这景象如画，几乎像一场海市蜃楼：南墙上的葡萄藤，一家现代公司借来的机器人吊车，水面上一楔形的光。托马斯用一片橡木夹板箍住一根开裂的柱子，压紧接口，又钉上一块纸盒般薄的木板来遮住接缝。他要消除每一处薄弱点，用铁把每根支柱环绕加固，降低坍塌的风险。他不愿成为这港口衰败的原因。

到那晚九点，工人们虽然醒着却已疲惫。隔壁大街上请来处理小伤口的一位外科医生，称赞了他们的测量和胆识。怀疑的毒药在这里没有立足之地。托马斯想起小镇昔日的商业辉煌，想起那些华丽的节庆、沿主街而行的婚礼队伍、人行道上一串串灯笼的痕迹。他要把小镇从腐朽中解放出来。他拿起喷嘴和水管去清理排水沟，往新木料上掀过一片帆布，尽管为过去多年那可耻的疏忽而感慨，却感到一种清晰而绚丽的希望。他知道，好手艺的语言无需翻译；它在每一码结实的木料中诉说着。机器人吊车嗡嗡作响；小伙子微笑了；港口将会活下去。

后来，在镇上的露天运动场里，市长发表了一通冗长的独白，有人称之为划时代之作，尽管一个直率的渔夫嘟囔道：「哦，去他的演讲吧。」托马斯——一个昔日的学徒，如今已是师傅——显得格外平静。他不愿做政治的评判者。他让自己的计划在纸上成形，在标着「到岸价」的一行里逐项列出进口钢材和铁矿石的成本，并克制住自己急躁的期望。一台自动锯在爬满葡萄藤的篱笆旁嗡嗡作响。一位邻居用一台绞肉机准备烤肉的填料，托马斯则要回家做一顿简单的晚饭。他把一件破旧的衣服紧紧搂在胸前，抵御夜里的寒意。这不是虚构，不是对梦想的空洞加工；这是实实在在的活儿，而且是好活儿。
